title: ServiceClient typed as `any` — all Supabase DB results are untyped
evidence: Line 164: `export type ServiceClient = any`. Every Edge Function that calls `supabase.from('rooms').select('*')` gets back `any` for both `data` and `error`. In submit_action/index.ts:107-109 the room row is stored in `data: any`, then properties like `room.players`, `room.active_player_index`, `room.turn`, `room.options` are read without any type guard. A schema change (column rename, missing field) would produce `undefined` at runtime with no TS compile error.
impact: Bug class: undetected schema drift. Any column rename or missing field on the DB row passes TypeScript compilation and fails silently at runtime — e.g. `room.active_player_index` becoming `null` instead of 0 causes `activeIndex` to be `NaN` (used in the modulo computation at line 137), which would misroute turn gating for every action in the room.
recommendation: Replace `ServiceClient = any` with the typed `SupabaseClient<Database>` generic from `@supabase/supabase-js`. Generate a `Database` type from the Supabase CLI (`supabase gen types typescript`) and import it. All `.from().select()` calls then return typed rows, so column access mismatches are compile errors.
acceptance_criteria: ServiceClient is typed as SupabaseClient<Database> or equivalent typed client
Accessing a non-existent column on a DB result produces a TS compile error

title: Fire-action casts `as number` / `as string` are real assertions, not coercions — partially backed by validate
evidence: After validateActionShape returns `{ok:true}`, the handler reassembles the `fire` branch with `a.angle as number`, `a.power as number`, `(a.weapon as string).trim()`. The `action` object's fields are typed as `unknown` in the destructuring (line 95). validateActionShape DOES check `typeof action.angle !== 'number' || !isFinite(action.angle)` (validate.ts:131) and the same for power (143) and weapon (150), so the casts ARE semantically backed. However TS cannot see that—the validator returns `{ok:true}` not a narrowed type—so any future change to `validateActionShape` (e.g. accidentally skipping the angle check) would silently break the cast without a TS error. The cast is sound today but fragile to refactor.
impact: Bug class: validator and usage can drift silently. If a future PR weakens validateActionShape's `fire` checks (e.g. drops the `isFinite` gate) the downstream `as number` cast at line 176 still compiles and the non-number value flows into the `NetworkFireAction` and ultimately into Postgres as a JSON value. Replay on every client then attempts physics with NaN angle/power and produces NaN positions — determinism broken.
recommendation: Extract a narrowed type from validateActionShape: change the return type from `ValidationResult` to a discriminated union that narrows the body on `ok:true` (a type predicate or a separate `ValidatedFireBody` type). Alternatively, re-derive a clean typed literal inside the `if (!shapeResult.ok) return` guard block using explicit runtime reads rather than casts. Either approach makes the coupling explicit to the compiler.
acceptance_criteria: The fire-branch construction uses no `as number` / `as string` casts
A weakened validator check causes a compile error at the usage site, not a runtime NaN

title: NetworkFireAction.weapon and NetworkBuyAction.weapon typed as `string`, not `WeaponType` — in shared/net/replay.ts
evidence: In shared/src/net/replay.ts line 28: `weapon: string` on NetworkFireAction; line 37: `weapon?: string` on NetworkBuyAction. When replayNetworkAction applies a fire action (line 117): `engine.applyAction({ type: 'select_weapon', weapon: action.weapon as WeaponType })` — the cast `as WeaponType` at replay time is necessary precisely because the logged type is `string`. An unrecognized weapon string (e.g. a future weapon type replayed against an older client, or a typo in a handcrafted action) passes the cast silently and reaches `getWeapon(type)` which does `WEAPONS[type]` — an unguarded record lookup that returns `undefined` on an unknown key, causing a crash when the engine tries to read `.detonation` on `undefined` (GameEngine.ts:1311).
impact: Bug class: unrecognized weapon string causes a runtime crash in every client that replays the action. Because the action log is permanent and replayed on every rejoin, one bad weapon string permanently bricks the room for all clients. `getWeapon` has no guard for unknown keys.
recommendation: 1) Strengthen NetworkFireAction.weapon to `WeaponType` and NetworkBuyAction.weapon to `WeaponType` in replay.ts. This catches the drift at the point the action is constructed (NetworkClient line 473, maybeDriveBot line 987). 2) Add a guard in `getWeapon()` (WeaponSystem.ts:589): `if (!(type in WEAPONS)) throw new Error(...)` so an unknown string fails fast with a useful message rather than crashing on `.detonation` access. 3) If backward-compat with persisted `string` rows is needed, add a type-guard function `isWeaponType(s: string): s is WeaponType`.
acceptance_criteria: NetworkFireAction.weapon and NetworkBuyAction.weapon are typed WeaponType (or the `string` is validated before the cast is removed)
getWeapon() throws a typed error on an unknown key rather than returning undefined
No `as WeaponType` cast required in replayNetworkAction

title: SupabaseTypes.RoomAction.action typed as NetworkFireAction only — excludes shield/buy/next_round
evidence: SupabaseTypes.ts line 30: `action: NetworkFireAction`. This type represents a `room_actions` row. But the log carries all four action types (fire, use_shield, buy, next_round). In NetworkClient.ts line 245, incoming Realtime rows are typed as `RoomActionRow` (a local interface that correctly uses `NetworkAction`), so the live path is fine. The exported `RoomAction` type in SupabaseTypes.ts is however stale and misleading — any future caller (or test) that imports `RoomAction` and reads `.action` believes it is always a `NetworkFireAction` with `.angle`, `.power`, `.weapon`, which is false for shield/buy/next_round rows.
impact: Bug class: callers importing `RoomAction` would get a false static guarantee that `.action.type === 'fire'` is always true and that `.action.angle` / `.action.weapon` are always present — masking missing type checks. A future component that switches on `action.type` would not get TS exhaustiveness checking because the union is not expressed.
recommendation: Update `SupabaseTypes.RoomAction.action` to use the full `NetworkAction` union (imported from `@shared/net/replay`). The file already imports `WeaponType` from shared, so importing `NetworkAction` is consistent with the existing pattern. Alternatively, delete the unused `RoomAction` export and the local `NetworkFireAction` interface if neither is imported outside this file.
acceptance_criteria: RoomAction.action is typed NetworkAction (the full union)
The local NetworkFireAction duplicate in SupabaseTypes.ts is removed
No new `as NetworkFireAction` casts introduced to compensate

title: Missing `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` — Record/array indexing silently returns T instead of T|undefined
evidence: All three tsconfig files extend tsconfig.base.json which sets `strict: true` but does NOT set `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`. The `strict` flag does NOT include noUncheckedIndexedAccess. Concrete examples where this matters: (1) `shared/src/engine/GameEngine.ts:328` — `WEAPONS[type]` in getWeapon returns `WeaponDefinition`, not `WeaponDefinition | undefined`; an unknown key returns `undefined` silently (see dx-003). (2) `shared/src/engine/Tank.ts:38` — `START_AMMO[weapon]` for a key not in the `Exclude<WeaponType,'baby_missile'>` union would return `undefined`. (3) GameEngine line 793 — `this.state.projectiles` array access via for-of is safe, but `tanks[(start + step) % n]` (line 1064) returns `TankState` not `TankState | undefined` per the compiler.
impact: Bug class: accessing a typed Record with an out-of-range or future-extended key returns `undefined` at runtime while the type system reports the element type. This has already led to the dx-003 issue (getWeapon on unknown weapon) and makes future weapon additions risky (a new WeaponType in the union but missing from WEAPONS would crash instead of erroring at compile time).
recommendation: Add `"noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true` to tsconfig.base.json. This will surface genuine footguns — address them by adding `?? default` or explicit guards. For getWeapon specifically, a non-null assertion after a type-guard is the right local fix; the tsconfig change ensures no future similar access slips through.
acceptance_criteria: All new Record/array indexing is written to handle the |undefined case
getWeapon handles the undefined case explicitly

title: GameEngine.armsLevel computed with `as number` cast inside a guard that cannot prove the type
evidence: Line 328: `this.armsLevel = Number.isFinite(al) ? clamp(Math.floor(al as number), 0, 4) : 4;` where `al = options?.armsLevel` typed as `number | undefined`. `Number.isFinite(undefined)` returns `false`, so the `al as number` in the truthy branch is correct in practice — but TypeScript cannot narrow `al` through `Number.isFinite` because `Number.isFinite` does not narrow its argument (it accepts `unknown` and returns `boolean`, not a type predicate narrowing to `number`). The cast silences TS without the compiler verifying correctness.
impact: Bug class: if `options.armsLevel` is ever changed to allow non-numeric values (e.g. a string from the lobby), `Number.isFinite` still returns false (correct), but the `as number` branch remains and would coerce the wrong type if someone reorders or inverts the ternary. Low probability, but a named footgun pattern.
recommendation: Replace `al as number` with a real narrowing: `typeof al === 'number' && Number.isFinite(al) ? clamp(Math.floor(al), 0, 4) : 4`. `typeof al === 'number'` narrows to `number` for the compiler, making the inner `Math.floor(al)` cast-free.
acceptance_criteria: The armsLevel computation uses no `as number` cast
A string value for options.armsLevel is handled by the else branch, not the cast branch

title: Lobby.ts response payloads cast with `as {field?: T}` — missing fields are silently undefined
evidence: Lobby.ts line 851: `const data = await res.json() as { roomId?: string; code?: string; playerId?: string; players?: NetworkPlayer[]; error?: string }`. The non-null assertions on lines 862-864 (`data.roomId!`, `data.code!`, `data.playerId!`) assert presence without any runtime check — if the server returns an unexpected shape (e.g. a 200 with a different payload structure), these `!` operators produce `undefined` values that flow into `this.waitingRoomId`, `this.waitingRoomCode`, `this.waitingPlayerId` as empty strings, silently breaking the waiting-room state. The same pattern appears in join_room and ready_up flows throughout Lobby.ts.
impact: Bug class: a malformed-but-200 server response causes silent `undefined`-as-string assignment, producing empty room IDs that pass subsequent guards (non-empty string checks) but cause misrouted Realtime subscriptions and failed room operations with no error displayed to the user.
recommendation: After the `!res.ok || data.error` guard, add an explicit shape guard: `if (!data.roomId || !data.code || !data.playerId) { this.onlineError = 'Unexpected server response'; ... return; }`. This removes the need for `!` assertions and gives the user a diagnostic instead of a silent failure.
acceptance_criteria: Non-null assertions on parsed response fields are replaced with runtime checks
A structurally wrong 200 response produces a visible error, not silent undefined propagation
